Internationalization (i18n)
===========================

RansomLook's web UI supports multiple languages through
`Flask-Babel <https://python-babel.github.io/flask-babel/>`_. The project currently
ships **English** (source language) and **French** translations.

How the locale is chosen
------------------------

On every request the active locale is picked in this order:

1. **``locale`` cookie** — set by the user when they click the ``EN / FR`` switcher
   in the top bar. Persisted for one year.
2. **Browser ``Accept-Language``** — when no cookie is set, the best match against
   the list of available locales is used.
3. **Fallback** — ``en``.

Switching language on demand
----------------------------

Hitting ``/set-locale/<code>`` (for example ``/set-locale/fr``) sets the cookie
to the requested locale and bounces back to the referrer. The top-bar switcher
is just a pair of links to those endpoints.

File layout
-----------

::

    babel.cfg                                   # extraction config (Python + Jinja2)
    messages.pot                                # extracted template catalogue
    translations/
        en/LC_MESSAGES/messages.po              # English source strings
        en/LC_MESSAGES/messages.mo              # compiled (binary)
        fr/LC_MESSAGES/messages.po              # French translations
        fr/LC_MESSAGES/messages.mo              # compiled (binary)

Flask-Babel loads the ``.mo`` files at process start. You **must restart** the
web server (gunicorn / development runner) after recompiling for changes to take effect.

Workflow for contributors
-------------------------

Extracting strings
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Whenever a template or Python file gains a new ``_('...')`` or ``gettext(...)`` call:

.. code-block:: bash

    poetry run pybabel extract -F babel.cfg -o messages.pot .

Updating existing catalogues
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Merge new strings into every language catalogue while preserving existing
translations (fuzzy matches are flagged, not overwritten):

.. code-block:: bash

    poetry run pybabel update -i messages.pot -d translations

Editing translations
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Open ``translations/<lang>/LC_MESSAGES/messages.po`` in any PO editor
(Poedit, Emacs po-mode, VS Code gettext plugins, …). Fill in the empty
``msgstr ""`` entries and remove the ``#, fuzzy`` flag once you have reviewed
the suggested translation.

Adding a new language
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Initialise a catalogue from the template:

.. code-block:: bash

    poetry run pybabel init -i messages.pot -d translations -l de

Then expose the new locale in ``website/web/__init__.py``:

.. code-block:: python

    AVAILABLE_LOCALES = ("en", "fr", "de")

and add a translation for the language name in the switcher (``base.html``).

Compiling
~~~~~~~~~

Before running the app, compile ``.po`` → ``.mo``:

.. code-block:: bash

    poetry run pybabel compile -d translations

Escaping ``%`` signs
--------------------

Flask-Babel / gettext uses Python's ``%``-formatting. Any literal percent sign
in a translatable string must be escaped as ``%%``. This is why
``Normalize (%%)`` appears in the templates rather than ``Normalize (%)``.
Failing to escape leads to ``ValueError: unsupported format character`` at
render time.

Plural forms
------------

Use ``ngettext`` (aliased as ``_()`` won't work for plurals):

.. code-block:: jinja

    {{ ngettext('%(n)s post', '%(n)s posts', count, n=count) }}

In the catalogue this yields ``msgid_plural`` and ``msgstr[0] / msgstr[1]`` entries.

Translating the documentation itself
====================================

The RansomLook documentation you are reading now is available in several
languages as well. Diagrams use neutral English labels; only the surrounding
prose is translated.

Local setup
-----------

The extensions required to build a translated documentation are listed in
``docs/requirements.txt`` (``sphinx-intl``). ``conf.py`` already exposes the
language through the ``SPHINX_LANGUAGE`` environment variable and points
Sphinx at ``docs/locale/`` for translation catalogues.

Extract and update catalogues
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

From the repository root:

.. code-block:: bash

    # extract every translatable chunk from the .rst files
    sphinx-build -b gettext docs docs/_build/gettext

    # merge the extracted template into the French catalogue (creates it on first run)
    sphinx-intl update -p docs/_build/gettext -d docs/locale -l fr

Edit the ``docs/locale/fr/LC_MESSAGES/*.po`` files. Any PO editor works
(Poedit, VS Code gettext, …).

Build a localised version
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

.. code-block:: bash

    # French build
    SPHINX_LANGUAGE=fr sphinx-build -b html docs docs/_build/html/fr

    # English build (default)
    sphinx-build -b html docs docs/_build/html/en

No compilation step is needed for ``.mo`` — Sphinx reads ``.po`` directly.

Wiring it up on Read the Docs
-----------------------------

Read the Docs has native support for `documentation translations
<https://docs.readthedocs.com/platform/stable/localization.html>`_. The
canonical setup is:

1. **Keep a single Git repository** (this one) hosting both the source files
   and the ``docs/locale/<lang>/`` catalogues.
2. **Create a "translation" project on RTD** for each language
   (e.g. ``ransomlook-fr``). Point it at the same Git repository and branch.
3. In the project's *Admin → Advanced settings*, set the **Language** to the
   target locale (``French``).
4. In the project's *Admin → Environment variables*, add
   ``SPHINX_LANGUAGE=fr`` so the build uses the French catalogue.
5. Open the **main** project (English). Go to *Admin → Translations* and
   **link the French project** as a translation. Read the Docs will then
   serve the docs at:

   - ``https://ransomlook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/`` (main, default)
   - ``https://ransomlook.readthedocs.io/fr/latest/`` (translated)

6. The language switcher in the RTD flyout menu picks this up automatically.

Repeat steps 2–5 per additional language.

Keeping translations in sync
----------------------------

Whenever a ``.rst`` file is modified:

1. Re-run ``sphinx-build -b gettext``.
2. Re-run ``sphinx-intl update`` — new or changed paragraphs show up as empty
   or fuzzy entries in the ``.po`` files.
3. Translate and commit.

Mermaid diagrams use English labels by convention, so they require no
translation work. If a diagram ever needs to carry localised text, drop
the label into the surrounding prose (which is translated) rather than
inside the diagram body.
